name: Aldo Moro Kidnapping
description: The Aldo Moro kidnapping refers to the abduction of the former Italian Prime Minister, Aldo Moro, on March 16, 1978, by the Red Brigades (Brigate Rosse), a far-left militant organization. Moro was a prominent figure in the Christian Democracy (DC) party and was instrumental in forging the Historic Compromise, an attempt to bring the Italian Communist Party (PCI) into the governing coalition. The Red Brigades held Moro captive for 55 days, during which they demanded the release of imprisoned comrades. The Italian government refused to negotiate, and Moro was ultimately murdered and his body left in the trunk of a car in Rome on May 9, 1978. While the official narrative attributes the kidnapping and murder solely to the Red Brigades, various conspiracy theories persist. These theories often implicate elements within the Italian state, intelligence agencies (both domestic and foreign, including the CIA), and even the Mafia, suggesting they either facilitated or allowed the kidnapping to occur to destabilize Italian politics and prevent the Historic Compromise. Proponents point to inconsistencies in the official investigation, alleged hidden agendas of political actors, and the presence of suspicious individuals and events surrounding the kidnapping. Mainstream scholarship largely refutes these theories, emphasizing the Red Brigades' ideological motivations and operational capabilities. Critics argue that conspiracy theories lack concrete evidence and often rely on speculation and misinterpretations of events. They maintain that the Red Brigades acted independently, driven by their revolutionary goals and a desire to strike at the heart of the Italian state.
subtitle: A Nation Gripped by Terror and Conspiracy

claims.claim: The CIA was involved in the kidnapping.
claims.evidence: Claims of CIA involvement stem from the agency's known anti-communist stance and alleged desire to prevent the Historic Compromise. Some point to suspicious activities of individuals linked to the CIA in Italy at the time.
claims.criticism: No direct evidence links the CIA to the kidnapping. Mainstream historians argue that blaming the CIA oversimplifies a complex political situation and ignores the Red Brigades' own agency and motivations.

claims.claim: Elements within the Italian state allowed the kidnapping to happen.
claims.evidence: Proponents point to alleged security lapses during the kidnapping and the government's refusal to negotiate with the Red Brigades, suggesting a calculated decision to sacrifice Moro.
claims.criticism: The government's refusal to negotiate was based on a policy of not yielding to terrorism. Security lapses are attributed to incompetence and the difficulty of anticipating such an audacious attack, not intentional complicity.

claims.claim: The Mafia played a role in Moro's death.
claims.evidence: Some theories suggest the Mafia was involved in the kidnapping or the disposal of Moro's body, possibly to destabilize the government or for their own political gain.
claims.criticism: While the Mafia's presence in Italy was undeniable, there is no credible evidence linking them directly to Moro's kidnapping or murder. Such claims are often based on speculation and circumstantial evidence.

keyFigures.name: Aldo Moro
keyFigures.role: Victim of the kidnapping and former Prime Minister

keyFigures.name: Mario Moretti
keyFigures.role: Leader of the Red Brigades during the kidnapping

keyFigures.name: Sergio Flamigni
keyFigures.role: Politician and proponent of conspiracy theories surrounding the kidnapping

keyFigures.name: Miguel Gotor
keyFigures.role: Historian who has extensively researched and written about the Moro kidnapping, refuting conspiracy theories

timeline.event: Aldo Moro is kidnapped by the Red Brigades
Moro's letters are published, revealing his desperate appeals to the government
Aldo Moro's body is found in Rome

sources.author: Sergio Flamigni
